# Brief: Tom (PERSONA-006)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-30

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence has emerged in the period between the March 23 budget workshop and the March 30 meeting.

The absence of news is itself a signal worth noting. The board adjourned on March 23 without taking a vote on the FY27 budget — and 72 employees have been carrying layoff notices since March 18. That's an active, unresolved personnel action sitting in the open. A week of public silence doesn't mean nothing is happening; it may mean the administration and board are in internal negotiations, or that the next meeting is intended as the decision point. Either way, Tom walks in knowing the March 23 workshop gave him the most complete causal account of the crisis he's heard all winter — the new finance director named the structural mechanism clearly — but the board still hasn't committed to a number, a vote, or a timeline.

The $8.4M deficit, the $257/year per-household cost of staying at 6%, the labor contract math that makes 6% unsustainable beyond FY27 — all of that is now on the record. What isn't on the record yet is any binding decision.

---

## Open Questions

Tom is walking into March 30 carrying every one of these:

- **When is the actual budget vote?** The board adjourned March 23 without one. Is March 30 the vote, or another workshop? If it's a vote, what's the number going in?

- **Does 6% still hold at $8.4M?** The deficit grew again — from $7.2M to $8.4M — after health insurance finalization. Has anyone confirmed the 78 position cuts are still sufficient to land at 6%, or is there a gap?

- **Where is the 82-position breakdown?** The administration promised the city council a breakdown of those 82 added positions at the February 10 joint workshop. It's been seven weeks. If they can't explain the hiring that caused this crisis, how do I trust their budget math going forward?

- **What's the actual plan for FY28?** The finance director said on March 23 that labor contracts structurally push costs beyond 6% annually. That means either someone renegotiates the contracts, or we're heading into another crisis in twelve months. I haven't heard anyone say what they're actually going to do about it.

- **Will the board adopt a minimum fund balance threshold policy?** This is the specific governance failure the finance director identified as the mechanism behind the entire crisis. Is anyone proposing a policy fix, or are we just absorbing the lesson and moving on?

- **Are the seven retirements staying unfilled?** That was offered as a concrete savings mechanism back in January. I want to know whether those positions are still open or whether they've been quietly backfilled.

- **Will the board respond to what the union president said?** The support staff union president put the admin-vs.-aide cuts asymmetry on the record at the last meeting. Is there any administrative reduction happening before this budget is voted, or are all 78 cuts landing on teachers and support staff?

- **What is the total bill?** School tax increase, sewer rate hikes, police/fire bond, Mahoney deferred to 2027. Nobody has added these up for an average homeowner. That number exists. I want it.

- **What are the legal consequences of two years of non-compliant school budgets?** The city auditor confirmed this in February. I've heard no follow-up.

---

## Agenda Implications

No agenda is available for the March 30 meeting. Based on where the process stands, here is what Tom should be prepared for:

**A budget vote is possible — or imminent.** The board has been in workshop mode since early winter. With the fiscal year ending June 30 and 72 employees in active layoff status, the pressure to produce a formal budget is acute. March 30 may be the meeting where the board votes on the FY27 budget, or it may be a final workshop before a vote at the next session. Either way, Tom should be prepared for a number to be formally proposed.

- **What to listen for:** Is the total budget increase stated as a percentage? Is the per-household cost ($257/year at 6%) restated clearly, or does the board present the number in a way that obscures the homeowner impact?
- **What to prepare:** If a vote is taken, Tom should know before he walks in that 6% was confirmed for FY27 on March 23, but that the board has a pattern of reframing the ceiling after appearing to accept it. Watch whether the motion matches what was said in workshop.

**Reconfiguration decisions may be finalized or deferred.** Nine options were presented on March 2, the highest-savings option (Skillin closure) was publicly vetoed before any vote, and no substitute of equivalent savings has been named. If March 30 includes a reconfiguration vote, it is likely to land on a lower-savings option — which means either the cuts deepen elsewhere or the 6% ceiling slips.

- **What to listen for:** Does any board member name the dollar gap left by rejecting the Skillin option? Does the administration identify what absorbs that gap?
- **What to prepare:** Ask or listen for whether the approved reconfiguration option closes the full $8.4M or requires additional cuts to achieve the 6% target.

**Layoff notice timelines may come up procedurally.** Maine public school layoff notices have legally required timelines. If 72 employees received notices on March 18, there may be procedural items on the agenda related to those notices — rescissions, confirmations, or union responses.

- **What to listen for:** Are any notices being rescinded, suggesting the budget math has shifted again? Are union representatives speaking to the procedural record?
- **What to prepare:** Any rescission without a named budget offset is a signal the 6% ceiling is softening.

---

## Watch For

- **Whether the board moves to a formal vote.** If March 30 is a vote night, watch whether the motion cites a specific dollar increase or only a percentage. A motion framed as "not to exceed 6%" has different enforceability than one that names a dollar figure.

- **Whether the 82-position breakdown finally appears.** Seven weeks overdue. If it's presented tonight — even in summary form — Tom should note whether it confirms the over-hiring narrative from March 23 or introduces revisions that soften the accountability picture.

- **Whether anyone from administration or the board addresses the FY28 structural impossibility.** The finance director named it explicitly on March 23: labor contracts alone exceed 6% annual cost growth. If no one has a stated plan for FY28 by the end of this meeting, that silence is Tom's strongest talking point going forward.

- **Whether the fund balance threshold policy is mentioned.** This is the specific governance fix the finance director identified. If it's not on tonight's agenda or mentioned as a coming agenda item, it may never appear — and Tom should be prepared to ask when it will.

- **The finance director.** This is the seventh finance director in six years and the first one who named the structural mechanism clearly on the record. Watch whether the board treats his analysis as directionally useful or as a constraint to be managed around. If the board moves in a direction that contradicts his March 23 analysis tonight, that contradiction matters.

- **Any mention of FY28 budget timelines or multi-year projections.** If the board presents only FY27 numbers without addressing the out-year math, that's the pattern that produced the current crisis. Tom should track whether anyone asks — or is asked — what happens in twelve months.

- **Whether any board member restates the combined per-household burden across all four pressures.** If Resident Chapman's unanswered question from March 5 still hasn't been addressed, Tom is the natural person to restate it at public comment. The number hasn't been produced. The question is legitimate and specific.

- **The tone of the vote, if there is one.** A unanimous or near-unanimous vote on a budget that required 78 layoff notices and hit homeowners at $257/year is a governance signal, not just a fiscal one. A fractured vote — particularly if the dissenters are the same members who have pushed above 6% before — tells Tom whether the February guidance actually held.

- **Whether any board member moves to exceed 6%.** Board member Feller signaled openness to exceeding the cap as early as January 12 and again on February 9. If that motion surfaces tonight, Tom should note which members support it and whether the motion includes any offset or is purely additive.
